## Security & Compliance

> ⚠️ LOCKED — These rules cannot be overridden.

### Injection Prevention

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Parameterized queries only
NEVER concatenate or interpolate user input into SQL, NoSQL, LDAP, or OS commands. ALWAYS use parameterized queries (`$1`, `?`, `:param`), prepared statements, or ORM bindings. Example: use `query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = $1', [id])` instead of `query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ' + id)`.

### Data Privacy & PII

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Never disable TLS/SSL verification
NEVER set `rejectUnauthorized: false`, `verify=False`, `InsecureSkipVerify: true`, or `NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0`. For local dev, use mkcert to create trusted certificates. Flag in code review as CRITICAL if found.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Encrypt sensitive data at rest and in transit
MUST use TLS 1.2+ for all network communication and AES-256+ for storage. Hash passwords with bcrypt, scrypt, or argon2 only—NEVER MD5/SHA. Example: use `bcrypt.hash(password, 10)` instead of custom hashing.

### Authorization & Access Control

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Authorization on every endpoint
MUST verify server-side that the authenticated user is authorized for the specific resource. Check ownership on `GET/PUT/DELETE /:id` and roles on admin endpoints. Example: `if(user.id !== resource.ownerId) throw 403`. Never rely on client-side guards alone—BOLA/IDOR is the #1 API vulnerability.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Never implement custom authentication
NEVER build custom password hashing, JWT validation, session management, or OAuth flows. MUST use the project's auth system or a proven provider (Auth0, Firebase, Cognito, Clerk). Custom auth introduces timing attacks and token vulnerabilities.

### Destructive Operations

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### No unguarded destructive operations
NEVER execute DELETE, DROP, `rm -rf`, `git push --force`, or `git reset --hard` without explicit user confirmation. Require idempotency: soft deletes preferred over hard deletes, rollback scripts mandatory for migrations. AI tools must never autonomously execute irreversible operations.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Database changes through migrations only
MUST route all schema changes through migration tools (Drizzle, Prisma, Alembic, Flyway). NEVER run ad-hoc SQL or direct ALTER TABLE outside migrations. Every migration requires a rollback script. Direct modifications bypass peer review and rollback capability.

### Error Exposure

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### No internal details in error responses
NEVER return stack traces, SQL, file paths, or database errors to clients. ALWAYS use: `{ "error": { "code": "ERROR_CODE", "message": "user-safe text", "requestId": "..." } }`. Route all errors through centralized handler.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### PII in logs
NEVER log, expose in API responses, or store unencrypted: passwords, SSNs, credit cards, email addresses, phone numbers, auth tokens. Redact with placeholders (e.g., `***REDACTED***`) or hash when auditing is needed. PII exposure violates compliance and enables identity theft.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### secrets management
NEVER commit secrets, API keys, or credentials to version control. MUST use environment variables or a secrets manager (e.g., .env.local, AWS Secrets Manager). Rotate exposed secrets immediately.
## Architecture & Patterns

### Error Handling

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### No silent error swallowing
NEVER use empty catch blocks, bare `except: pass`, or `.catch(() => {})`. Every catch block MUST handle (retry/fallback/user message), re-throw, or log with context. If safe to ignore, add a comment explaining why. Example: `catch(e) { logger.error('Payment retry failed', {error: e, userId}); notifyOps(); }` not `catch(e) {}`.

### Testing

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Tests required for new logic
MUST write tests for all new functions, endpoints, and behavioral changes. Minimum: happy path test, one error case, one boundary condition. Use the project's existing test framework and place tests in the established test directory. Code without tests is treated as untested and subject to removal.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### input validation
MUST validate all inputs (query params, request body, URL segments) with Zod schemas before processing. Example: `z.object({ reference_id: z.string().uuid() })`. Reject invalid inputs with 400 status and schema error details. Never trust client data.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### repository pattern
MUST route all database access through repository classes (e.g., `WebhookRepository`, `ChargeRepository`). NEVER call Prisma directly in service code. DO: inject repositories as dependencies.
## Conventions

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### structured logging
ALWAYS log as JSON with fields: timestamp, level, message, context (userId, requestId, operation). Example: `{"timestamp": "2024-01-15T10:30:00Z", "level": "error", "message": "User auth failed", "userId": "u123"}`. Never log plain text or omit request IDs.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### tailwind css usage
MUST use Tailwind CSS classes exclusively — NEVER use inline styles. Example: `className="fixed inset-0 bg-black/50 flex items-center justify-center"` not `style={{...}}`. See .cursorrules for Tailwind configuration.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### error response format
ALWAYS return errors as `{ error: { code: "ERROR_CODE", message: "description" } }`. Example: `{ error: { code: "MISSING_PARAMETER", message: "reference_id is required" } }`. See API standards for full spec.
